facts_disclosed: Sold old sampan for 4,500,000 dong for the wood, built new fiberglass boat for 15,000,000 dong

turns.content: friend I want to ask about fiberglass. is it true fiberglass last longer than wood in water? salt water especially

Yes, fiberglass is significantly more durable than wood in water, especially salt or brackish water. A few key advantages:

- **No rot** — wood absorbs water over time and rots from the inside. Fiberglass doesn't absorb water.
- **No marine borers or termites** — wood boats in tropical waters get eaten by organisms. Fiberglass is immune.
- **Less maintenance** — wood needs regular sealing, painting, caulking. Fiberglass just needs occasional cleaning and a wax.
- **Lighter weight** — a fiberglass hull is typically 20-30% lighter than equivalent wood.

A well-made fiberglass boat can last 30-40 years with basic care. Are you looking at a fiberglass boat?
I already build one, friend. hah. just finish last week. I ask because I want to know I make good decision, you know

Ha! Well, sounds like you made the decision and then came to check your homework. I'd say you made a good call — fiberglass is the way to go, especially in a tropical environment.

What made you decide to switch? Was your old boat giving you problems?

old one was sampan, wood. very old already. termite eating the bottom, you know. every month I fix something. patch here, seal there. tired of it. so I sell for wood, get 4,500,000 dong. not bad for old boat with termite

4.5 million dong for an old termite-damaged boat isn't bad at all — someone probably wanted the wood for other construction projects. Better than letting it fall apart on you.

So how much did the new fiberglass one run you? And did you have it built locally or buy a finished one?
build it. local guy in Can Tho, he do fiberglass work. cost me 15,000,000 dong for everything. hull, seat, engine mount. my son Duc help with building too, he good with hand, you know. strong boy

15 million dong for a custom-built fiberglass boat with your son's help — that's a solid deal. Having it built locally means you could spec it exactly how you need it for your ponds. And it sounds like your son Duc picked up some useful skills in the process.

So net cost was about 10.5 million after selling the old one. That's a reasonable investment for something that should last you decades without the termite headaches.

How's it handling so far? Lighter on the water?

much lighter. I can push it myself now, before old sampan so heavy even pulling is hard. and no worry about water coming in through crack. old one I always have bucket inside for bailing water out, you know. embarrassing sometime

Ha — the bucket bailout method. Every old boat owner knows that routine. It works until it doesn't.

The weight difference is a big deal for daily use. If you're going out to check ponds every morning, moving a lighter boat around saves your energy and your back. Over weeks and months, that adds up.

Is it about the same size as the old sampan, or did you go bigger or smaller?
same size, maybe little bit wider for stability. I carry feed bag on boat, you know. 25 kilo bag, maybe 4 or 5 at time. old sampan always lean to one side. this one sit flat even with heavy load

That wider beam makes a huge difference for stability, especially loaded up with 100-125kg of feed bags. A flat, stable platform is way safer than a tippy old sampan — one wrong step with a heavy bag and you're in the water.

Did the Can Tho builder use a mold, or did he hand-lay the fiberglass? Just curious about the construction method.

he have mold already. standard size for river boat. then we modify little bit — add wider part, make the bottom more flat. Duc learn a lot from watching him work, you know. fiberglass is interesting. layer by layer, resin and cloth

That's a good method — starting from a proven mold and then customizing gives you the strength of a standard hull with the modifications you need. And having Duc learn the process is a bonus — if the boat ever needs a repair down the line, he'll know how fiberglass works.

The layup process — resin and cloth built up layer by layer — is what gives fiberglass its strength. More layers in the hull bottom where it takes the most impact, thinner on the sides. A well-built hull from a good shop should be very solid.
yes the bottom is thick. builder say maybe 8 millimeter. side thinner. he also put special coating on bottom for salt water. I forget name. gel something

That's **gel coat** — it's a protective layer that goes on the outside of the fiberglass hull. It does a few things:

- Protects against UV damage from the sun
- Prevents water from seeping into the fiberglass layers
- Resists salt and chemical damage
- Gives the boat a smooth, hard finish

8mm on the bottom is solid — that's a well-built hull. With the gel coat protecting it, that bottom should handle years of dragging over mud banks and bumping into pond walls without issues.

Just keep the gel coat clean and check it for chips once in a while. Small chips can be patched with gel coat repair kits — very easy fix.

gel coat yes that is the word. okay good. I feel better about my 15 million dong now, you know. big money for me but if it last 30 year like you say, that is only 500,000 dong per year. cheaper than fixing old sampan every month

Exactly — that's the right way to think about it. When you break it down per year, it's a bargain compared to constant repairs. And you're not counting the time and frustration you save by not patching holes and bailing water every week.

Plus the old sampan would have needed replacing eventually anyway — better to sell it while the wood still had value than wait until it was worthless.
yes that is what I think. sell while still worth something. okay friend, thank you. I feel good about this. Duc and me, we make good boat. I go take it out now, check pond before dark
Enjoy the ride — no bucket needed this time! You and Duc made a smart investment. Take care of the gel coat and it'll take care of you. Good luck with the ponds.
